children.name: Esox
children.description: Pike and muskellunge — the apex freshwater predators of the Northern Hemisphere, torpedo-shaped ambush hunters with a flattened, duck-like bill lined with hundreds of backward-pointing teeth. The northern pike (gädda) is the dominant large predator in virtually every Swedish lake and river. Pike are solitary and fiercely territorial, lying motionless in weed beds before lunging at prey with explosive acceleration. They will take fish more than half their own body length, and occasionally ducks, muskrats, and even small otters. In Sweden, pike fishing is a national passion — the spring pike season opening is a social event rivalling the crayfish season. The largest pike are old females; females grow much larger than males throughout their lives.

children.name: Esox lucius
children.description: Sweden's most iconic freshwater sport fish (gädda). Present in virtually every Swedish lake and slow river. Can reach 25 kg and over a metre in length. A keystone predator whose presence structures entire lake ecosystems. The female is substantially larger than the male; fish over 10 kg are almost invariably female.

children.name: Esox masquinongy
children.description: The largest member of the family — the legendary 'muskie' of North American sport fishing. Can exceed 1.8 m and 30 kg. Known as 'the fish of ten thousand casts' for its elusiveness. Hybridises with northern pike to produce the fertile 'tiger muskie'.

children.name: Esox reichertii
children.description: The largest and most distinctive pike, native to the Amur River basin of China and Russia. Covered with small, regular dark spots like a leopard. Can reach 115 cm. Its range does not overlap with northern pike, making it the apex freshwater predator of the Amur.

children.name: Esox americanus
children.description: The smallest North American esocid, reaching only about 40 cm, found in sluggish, heavily vegetated streams and ponds of the eastern United States. Two subspecies are recognised — the redfin pickerel (E. a. americanus) with bright red-orange fins, and the grass pickerel (E. a. vermiculatus) with yellow-green fins from the Great Lakes drainage. Both ambush invertebrates and small fish from cover among aquatic vegetation.

children.name: Esox niger
children.description: A medium-sized pickerel of the eastern United States and southeastern Canada, distinguished by the distinctive chain-link or reticulated pattern on its golden-green sides. Favours clear, weedy lakes and slow rivers, and is a popular sport fish prized for its aggressive strike. The largest of the North American pickerels, reaching about 80 cm; closely related to the northern pike and occasionally hybridises with it.

children.name: Esox cisalpinus
children.description: A pike species endemic to the Po Valley and neighbouring river basins of northern Italy, Switzerland, and Slovenia, long treated as a southern population of the northern pike but recognised as a distinct species based on genetic and morphological evidence. Distinguished from Esox lucius by a more uniform greenish coloration with reduced or absent yellow mottling and different scale and lateral-line counts. Threatened by hybridisation with the introduced northern pike.

children.name: Esox aquitanicus
children.description: A recently described pike species native to rivers of southwestern France (the Garonne and Adour drainages) and the northern Iberian Peninsula, described on the basis of molecular data in 2014. Like the southern pike it was previously treated as part of Esox lucius, but shows clear genetic distinctiveness. Its recognition highlights how the pike genus in Europe is more diverse than historically appreciated, with several lineages isolated by Pleistocene glacial barriers.
